coredata.dc:description: Rising heat fluxes in wide-bandgap power electronics and data center hardware have renewed interest in single-phase immersion cooling (SPIC) as a simple, scalable alternative to indirect liquid cooling as well as two-phase immersion cooling systems. However, the lack of standardized, property-based liquid coolant benchmarks has complicated engineering decisions. This study offers a comprehensive evaluation of the thermal and electrical performance of various dielectric liquids for SPIC of electronic devices. An analysis framework was developed using a figure of merit (FOM) based on natural convection correlations, allowing for property sensitivity and weight-factor assessments across different operating liquid temperatures. A buoyancy-driven SPIC experimental platform, featuring a commercial super junction (SJ) silicon MOSFET (drain tab size 10 mm × 12 mm), was built to measure the cooling and electrical insulation performance of 10 commercial dielectric liquids and 5 candidate chemistries not yet available commercially. Results reveal that high fluid thermal conductivity and density strongly improve cooling performance, while dynamic viscosity primarily limits it. Experimental data categorize the tested liquids into three tiers of cooling effectiveness: four commercial liquids in tier 3 with lowest junction-to-coolant thermal resistances of 7.0 – 7.8 K/W (3.27 – 3.75 (cm2ˑK)/W), seven in tier 2 with thermal resistances of 5.9 - 6.4 K/W (2.82 – 3.08 (cm2ˑK)/W), and four candidate liquids in tier 1 achieving the lowest resistances of 4.9 - 5.4 K/W (2.32 – 2.56 (cm2ˑK)/W). Electrical insulation tests show most liquids maintain leakage currents below 5 nA at 550 V. These findings offer clear guidelines for dielectric liquid selection, supporting SPIC deployment in data centers, battery thermal management systems, and power electronics applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensitivity analysis for mineral oil MO−24. The temperature reference for thermal properties is set as 60⁰C in the analysis. Data points represent calculations with lines between data points representing guiding lines only.

Physical property comparison between MO−24 (red circles) and HFE−7700 (blue dashed line) showing: (a) liquid density, (b) liquid thermal conductivity, (c) liquid dynamic viscosity (logarithmic scale), and (d) liquid specific heat capacity, as functions of fluid temperature (Tf). The legend in (a) is applicable to (b), (c), and (d).
Weight factor change as a function of liquid temperature (Tf) for different physical properties for (a) MO−24, and (b) HFE−7700. The y-axis of (b) is the same as the y-axis of (a).
Not-to-scale schematic of the experimental setup showing the immersion tank with the stainless-steel coiled heat exchanger inserted for temperature control.

(a) Schematic of the Infineon device package used for powering and heating. (b) CAD image of the custom PCB designed to heat up the device with the trace layouts labeled. (c) Photograph of the PCB with the soldered Infineon device on it in the bottom right corner.
Device temperature calibration data from two independent board device combinations with a linear fit (dotted line) representing the user-defined calibration curve (see Eq. 10).
Schematic of the current leakage test diagram. Schematic not to scale.

Fluid characteristics of the tested dielectrics. Thermal resistance (Rt, Eq. 15) as a function of cooling liquid characteristic temperature. Uncertainty in the measured temperature difference and input power are smaller than the symbol sizes and are not shown for clarity. Lines connecting the data points are guiding lines only, and do not represent any statistical fit. See Table 1 for fluid symbol definition.

Estimated heat transfer coefficient on the copper drain tab as a function of junction-to-fluid temperature difference or all 15 dielectric fluids. Lines connecting the data points are guiding lines only, and do not represent any statistical fit. See Table 1 for fluid symbol definition.

Figure of merit comparison of the results: (a) Theoretical FOM derived from Eq. (5) vs the inverse of thermal resistance data for selective fluids, (b) Theoretical FOM derived from Eq. (5) vs the derived experimental FOM of corresponding fluids. See Table 1 for fluid symbol definition.
Transient current leakage data for the SPHO liquid.

Current leakage level comparison between dielectric fluids. See Table 1 for full details of the dielectric fluid properties. Uncertainty in the current leakage measurement was ± 0.05 nA, and is not plotted for clarity.
Thermophysical properties of tested dielectric fluids at 25°C. Values reported at indicated temperature when 25°C data is unavailable.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rising heat fluxes in wide-bandgap power electronics and data center hardware have renewed interest in single-phase immersion cooling (SPIC) as a simple, scalable alternative to indirect liquid cooling as well as two-phase immersion cooling systems. However, the lack of standardized, property-based liquid coolant benchmarks has complicated engineering decisions. This study offers a comprehensive evaluation of the thermal and electrical performance of various dielectric liquids for SPIC of electronic devices. An analysis framework was developed using a figure of merit (FOM) based on natural convection correlations, allowing for property sensitivity and weight-factor assessments across different operating liquid temperatures. A buoyancy-driven SPIC experimental platform, featuring a commercial super junction (SJ) silicon MOSFET (drain tab size 10 mm × 12 mm), was built to measure the cooling and electrical insulation performance of 10 commercial dielectric liquids and 5 candidate chemistries not yet available commercially. Results reveal that high fluid thermal conductivity and density strongly improve cooling performance, while dynamic viscosity primarily limits it. Experimental data categorize the tested liquids into three tiers of cooling effectiveness: four commercial liquids in tier 3 with lowest junction-to-coolant thermal resistances of 7.0 – 7.8 K/W (3.27 – 3.75 (cm2ˑK)/W), seven in tier 2 with thermal resistances of 5.9 - 6.4 K/W (2.82 – 3.08 (cm2ˑK)/W), and four candidate liquids in tier 1 achieving the lowest resistances of 4.9 - 5.4 K/W (2.32 – 2.56 (cm2ˑK)/W). Electrical insulation tests show most liquids maintain leakage currents below 5 nA at 550 V. These findings offer clear guidelines for dielectric liquid selection, supporting SPIC deployment in data centers, battery thermal management systems, and power electronics applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The past decade has witnessed a dramatic increase in the power density of electronic systems, driven by breakthroughs in computing and the growing scale of data infrastructure [1,2]. Advanced artificial intelligence (AI) workloads and high-performance computing (HPC) applications require processors and accelerators with ever-higher performance, which in turn generate more heat per unit area compared to earlier generations [3,4]. Analogous trends are observed in electric vehicles, where growing energy demands of power electronics and high-capacity battery systems pose challenges to thermal and electrical management [5,6]. Rising energy throughput, decentralized generation, and smarter grid demands impose complex thermal and dielectric stresses on high voltage transformers, prompting the development of advanced modeling and cooling strategies to improve reliability and efficiency [7,8].

Heat fluxes at the chip level have risen into the tens of Watts per square centimeter for current processors, with projections of future devices potentially exceeding 1 kW/cm² [9]. This steep upward trend in power density underscores a fundamental reality: removing heat has become as critical as computing itself in modern system design [10]. The consumption of computing infrastructure has surged, with global data centers already drawing approximately 415 TWh per year, accounting for 1.5% of the world's electricity [11]. In extreme cases, exascale supercomputers require 20–30 MW of power for efficient operation, with traditional cooling systems constituting up to 40% of the total electricity usage [12,13]. Increasing compute power density is pushing conventional thermal management techniques to their limits, as every Watt consumed ultimately becomes heat that must be dissipated, raising operational costs and threatening long-term system sustainability [14,15]. If heat is not efficiently removed, even brief operation at high loads can create local hotspots that drive junction temperatures beyond safe limits, degrading performance and decreasing device reliability [16]. Thus, maintaining safe temperatures amid steadily rising power dissipation now serves as a primary bottleneck for sustaining performance growth in electronics [17,18].

Air cooling, the main solution for decades, is increasingly inadequate in the face of these high power densities [19,20]. Traditional air-cooled heat sinks and fan systems rely on convection heat transfer to the surrounding air. However, the low thermal conductivity and heat capacity of air make it a poor coolant for high heat flux scenarios [21,22]. There is a practical upper limit to how much heat can be expelled by forced air, roughly around 30–45 kW per data center 42 U server rack [23]. Pushing further would require impractically large heat sink assemblies, very high airflow rates which would necessitate noisy, high-power fans and chilled air temperatures far below ambient. All of these approaches are unsustainable or inefficient. To cope with this thermal issue, attention has turned to liquid cooling technologies as a next-generation solution [24]. Compared to air, liquids have orders of magnitude higher volumetric heat capacity and greater thermal conductivity, allowing them to absorb and transport much more heat with a given flow [25]. By circulating a coolant in direct contact with hot components or their heat spreaders, liquid cooling can remove heat far more efficiently, enabling stable operation at heat loads that would overwhelm air cooling [26].

Over the years, liquid cooling methods have evolved in form and application. Early and modern studies on direct-to-chip liquid cooling have demonstrated that attaching cold plates or microchannel heat exchangers directly to processors significantly reduces thermal resistance by enabling efficient convective heat removal using water or water-glycol mixtures. Kandlikar and Hayner [27] established foundational thermal design principles for liquid-cooled cold plates, showing substantial improvements in heat removal capability compared to air cooling. Subsequent system-level investigations confirmed that cold plate cooling enables higher rack power densities while maintaining acceptable junction temperatures in computing hardware [28], whereas related applications in electrochemical and energy systems demonstrated similar advantages in managing localized heat generation through controlled liquid circulation [29]. Comprehensive reviews of single-phase and two-phase heat transfer enhancement techniques further quantified how microstructured channels and optimized flow architectures improve heat transfer coefficients and overall cooling effectiveness [30,31]. More recent developments employing advanced manufacturing approaches, including etched large footprint cold plates and additively manufactured fin architectures, have reported ultra-low thermal resistance and improved thermal-hydraulic performance suitable for next generation high power electronics [32–34]. Liquid cooling (through a cold plate) using water can achieve very low thermal resistance from chip to coolant, but it requires complex plumbing infrastructure and meticulous engineering safeguards since water is electrically conductive and can cause current leakage and shorting of electronics if a leak is present in the system [35].

An alternative approach is immersion cooling that fully submerges electronic components in an electrically non-conductive liquid, allowing the fluid to flow directly over and around all heat-dissipating surfaces for effective thermal management [36,37]. Because the liquid is in contact with components, heat is removed with minimal thermal resistance, and even irregularly shaped or densely packed assemblies can be cooled uniformly [38]. Reviews of immersion-cooled systems further report reductions in thermal resistance pathways because heat is transferred directly from component surfaces to the surrounding dielectric liquid rather than through intermediate interfaces [39]. Experimental demonstrations of water-based immersion cooling have further shown that direct liquid contact can sustain high heat flux operation while maintaining stable device temperatures, highlighting the potential performance ceiling achievable when electrical insulation challenges are addressed through protective packaging strategies [40]. In parallel, recent system-level investigations integrating data-driven and machine learning based control frameworks have illustrated how immersion cooling platforms can be dynamically optimized to improve thermal regulation and operational efficiency in electrified energy systems [41]. In addition, immersion cooling systems operate with minimal noise and no airborne dust since they eliminate high-speed fans and open-air circulation. This yields quieter operation, a cleaner environment for electronics, and potentially improved hardware longevity [42]. In addition, the heat absorbed by the liquid can be readily transferred to external heat exchangers for waste heat recovery, such as repurposing the heat for building heating, further enhancing the sustainability of this approach [43,44]. These benefits have led to a surge of interest in immersion cooling across various industries.

The two primary variants of immersion cooling technology, distinguished by how the coolant handles the heat, are single-phase and two-phase immersion cooling [45]. In single-phase immersion cooling (SPIC), a low dielectric constant (typically less than 2.5) coolant remains in the liquid state throughout the process with no phase change occurring [46]. As components heat up, the surrounding liquid warms but does not evaporate or boil [47]. The heated liquid is circulated by natural convection (buoyancy-driven SPIC) or is pumped within the immersion tank to further enhance single phase convection. The liquid is eventually pumped to an external heat exchanger where the heat is rejected to a secondary cooling loop (pump-driven SPIC) or outdoor air (dry or wet cooling), and the cooled liquid then returns to the immersion cooling tank [48]. Since no boiling occurs, the system design is relatively simple, and the liquid stays chemically stable over a wide operating range [49]. Single-phase immersion setups are straightforward to implement and generally use inexpensive dielectric liquids such as mineral oils [50]. However, their cooling performance is ultimately limited by the sensible heat capacity and other thermal properties of the liquid, which constrain how much heat can be carried away for a given flow rate [51].

Two-phase immersion cooling (2PIC), by contrast, relies on boiling of the coolant to dissipate heat. The dielectric fluid is selected to have a low boiling point typically ranging from 30°C to 75°C, so that heat from the electronics causes the liquid to vaporize at the component surfaces [52]. This phase change absorbs a large amount of heat, effectively clamping component temperatures near the boiling point of the liquid [53,54]. The vapor then rises and condenses on a condenser integrated into the tank, releasing heat to a secondary loop. By leveraging latent heat, two-phase immersion achieves extremely high heat transfer coefficients and maintains very uniform component temperatures [55]. However, the dielectric fluids used in 2PIC require superior system sealing due to their high volatility and very low surface tension [56,57]. Beyond volatility, fluorinated two-phase liquids can act as aggressive solvents and plasticizers for common server and printed circuit board (PCB) materials like thermal greases, PVC wire insulation, and solder flux, leading to extractables that deposit on boiling sites, foul surfaces, and degrade long-term reliability [58]. Additionally, many low boiling point dielectric fluids have poor sustainability, high global warming potential (high-GWP), and high cost compared to SPIC fluids. Also, regulatory momentum around per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) is accelerating, as most commercially deployed 2PIC working fluids are fluorochemicals. Major suppliers of PFAS fluids (such as 3 M) are exiting the market, creating supply-chain and qualification risks [59,60]. In addition, there are no commercially adopted hydrocarbon dielectrics with suitably low boiling points and acceptable safety characteristics for 2PIC, whereas SPIC coolants are predominantly hydrocarbon liquids which avoid these PFAS issues [61]. Moreover, two-phase systems face dryout issues at the critical heat flux (CHF), posing a cooling risk under high power [62]. Thus, although 2PIC systems demonstrate strong potential, these limitations hinder their widespread adoption. In comparison, SPIC has gained greater commercial traction, particularly in data centers and cryptocurrency mining, where cost effectiveness and reliability outweigh the ultimate performance ceiling.

Despite the progress and promise of SPIC, one key open question is the choice of coolant. A wide variety of single phase dielectric liquid coolant options exist, ranging from mineral oils and synthetic hydrocarbon oils to natural esters and engineered fluorochemicals, each with distinct thermophysical and electrical properties [39,63]. These SPIC liquids differ in viscosity, thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, dielectric strength, dielectric constant, and chemical stability among other factors. The role of liquid chemistry is also overlooked in determining how composition influences the physical properties, and how chemical aging affects the liquid performance [64,65]. Comparative data on different liquids under identical conditions are scarce because much of the existing literature documents immersion cooling results for only a single chosen liquid under different testing conditions. Additionally, the coolant must efficiently transfer heat while also remaining electrically inert and chemically compatible with the electronics. Equally important but often omitted in SPIC discussions is flammability. Flash point places a practical limit on thermal performance because liquids with lower viscosity and higher heat transfer potential typically exhibit lower flash points. This flammability performance trade-off provides important context for why certain chemistries are not widely adopted in practice, in addition to the electrical and material compatibility considerations. In practice, this balance involves trade-offs; thus, rigorous evaluation of both thermal performance and electrical compatibility for candidate liquids is critical [66,67], which, however, remains scarce in previous studies. Most studies focus solely on the thermal and flow behavior without considering electrical degradation, leakage current, or voltage stress effects [64,68]. Previous research largely relies on simplified setups that fail to capture the dynamic thermal-electrical interactions that occur under realistic workloads during immersion [68,69]. Furthermore, long-term stability and material compatibility remain critical yet underexplored. Prolonged exposure of electronics or other polymer compounds in certain dielectric liquids poses a risk of performance degradation and electronics malfunction [70,71]. These challenges highlight the need for a multidisciplinary approach to SPIC research that accounts for thermal, electrical, chemical, and reliability factors in tandem.

In this work, a systematic comparative analysis of single-phase immersion cooling is carried out using a comprehensive list of 15 dielectric liquids featuring a variety of different chemistry compositions, with the aim of quantifying how the choice of liquid influences both cooling effectiveness and electrical compatibility. Multiple liquids were compared in a controlled setup that integrated a real-world PCB-mounted SJ silicon MOSFET electronic device. The study investigated both the thermal and electrical performance of the dielectric liquids, illustrating how various liquid properties translate into real-world cooling outcomes and equipment reliability. The findings are intended to guide the selection of optimal dielectric liquids for future immersion-cooled systems, helping engineers balance thermal management requirements with electrical and material compatibility considerations.

Since most electronic boards are placed vertically [40,61,66] in coolants in immersion cooling systems for easy access, the cooling performance is determined by the natural convection on vertical surfaces in a buoyancy-driven SPIC system. The heat transfer coefficient h , which is a measure of the rate at which heat is transferred per unit surface area per unit temperature difference between the solid device surface and the immersion liquid, can be expressed as:

While Eq. (4) describes the heat transfer coefficient for a specific geometry and operating condition, direct comparison between different immersion coolants using this expression is not straightforward because the heat transfer coefficient depends on system dimensions and temperature differences in addition to fluid properties. To enable a generalized comparison between candidate dielectric liquids, a figure of merit (FOM) is introduced by removing constants, geometric parameters, and temperature-dependent terms from the heat transfer coefficient. This approach isolates the contribution of intrinsic thermophysical properties to buoyancy-driven heat transfer performance and allows fluid selection to be evaluated independent of a particular device configuration. Based on the heat transfer coefficient, the figure of merit (FOM) is defined by removing constants, geometry, and temperatures as the following:
(5)

The FOM shows that the immersion cooling performance in a buoyancy-driven SPIC system is determined by the five physical properties of the liquid coolant, namely its density ρ , dynamic viscosity μ , specific heat capacity
c
p
, thermal conductivity k , and thermal expansion coefficient β . By expressing performance solely in terms of fluid properties, the FOM provides a physics-based framework for comparing different dielectric fluids tested in this study and for interpreting experimentally observed cooling trends in relation to fundamental transport mechanisms. This formulation yields a non-dimensional value based on SI units, and is consistent with previously reported convection-based FOM analyses used for coolant evaluation and fluid screening in the literature [73–75].

A sensitivity analysis based on FOM is carried out to examine how the change in the independent property of a liquid immersion coolants impacts the FOM under given assumptions, therefore determining the driving factors of the cooling performance. The variance of the FOM due to a property change can be calculated by,
(6)

A commonly used single-phase heat transfer fluid, MO−24, a high-purity white mineral oil (24 cSt at 40 ℃ ), is used as an example for sensitivity analysis, and the results are given in Fig. 1. The temperature reference for thermal properties is set as 60 ℃ , and each property was independently varied while the remaining properties were held constant. The sensitivity sweep separates the liquid properties into three groups. Thermal conductivity and density have the strongest positive impact. Doubling either increases the FOM by approximately 60%, while halving reduces it by approximately 40%. Their overlap highlights that conductivity and buoyancy contribute equally to increasing the Rayleigh number. Thermal expansion coefficient and specific heat capacity form a moderate tier. Doubling either increases the FOM by approximately 25%, and halving reduces the FOM by 20%. Their influence comes mainly from buoyancy and thermal storage. Dynamic viscosity acts oppositely. Doubling the dynamic viscosity cuts the FOM by 20%, whereas reducing it by 90% nearly doubles the performance. Overall, the analysis indicates that optimal immersion coolants should combine high liquid thermal conductivity, liquid density, liquid expansion coefficient and liquid heat capacity, while minimizing liquid dynamic viscosity.

Note that each property may have a different range of changes when comparing different liquids. For example, dynamic viscosity between different fluids could vary in the 1–2 orders of magnitude at the same temperature, while the thermal conductivity difference between most dielectric fluids only varies between 1–2-fold. Moreover, each property also exhibits different dependence on temperature for different fluids. To further evaluate the impact of physical properties on the immersion cooling performance, a weight-factor framework is used with consideration of temperature dependence. Based on Eq. Another commonly used dielectric fluid, HFE−7700, with distinctly different physical properties, is used to compare with MO−24. Chemically, mineral oils like MO−24 are a complex mixture of various hydrocarbon compounds derived from petroleum, while HFE fluids are fluorinated organic compounds. Due to the uniquely different molecular structures, mineral oils generally have high thermal conductivity, making them suitable candidates for heat transfer fluids. However, they exhibit higher viscosity than HFE fluids, limiting their performance in immersion cooling systems. Fig. 2 is presented as an illustrative comparison between the two representative dielectric fluid classes to demonstrate how temperature-dependent thermophysical properties influence the weight-factor framework introduced in Eqs. (7, 8). These fluids are not used to establish the general conclusions of this study but rather to provide physically representative examples for interpreting the analytical framework prior to applying it to the broader set of dielectric fluids investigated experimentally. Fig. 2 shows the comparison of physical properties between MO−24 and HFE−7700 with temperatures ranging from 0 to 150°C. The thermal expansion coefficients, which are often not provided by the manufacturers, are estimated based on temperature-dependent density data as below,

The figure shows that viscosity dominates the FOM at low temperatures for both fluids, about 70–80% at 0°C, but its influence declines with temperature as the contribution is reallocated to the other properties. Fig. 4 shows a schematic of the buoyancy-driven SPIC experimental setup. It consists of a 6-inch cubic glass tank (Marina Cubus Glass Betta Kit, Rolf C. Hagen Inc.) with 80% volume filled with the test dielectric liquid. A custom designed PCB with a mounted electronic device was vertically immersed in the fluid under test. Specifics of the PCB and device are discussed in the next section. A 3D-printed holder is used to install the board in the center of the tank to ensure consistent positioning. The distance between the device and the fluid level is approximately 6 cm. When the electronic device is powered, the heat generated from the device dissipates into the dielectric fluid through natural convection. To ensure consistent conditions across different fluids, the fluid bath temperature was maintained at around room temperature (20°C) using a stainless-steel coiled heat exchanger (lab-made coil from 0.25in stainless tube) surrounding the PCB. The coil is connected to a cooling loop driven by a circulating pump (Type RS 15–6, ZeroPone) and a chiller (LM61GX1A110C LM6 Benchtop Chiller, PolyScience). Two T-type thermocouples (Thermocouple Beaded Wire Sensor Type T 40 Gage, Evolution Sensors and Controls, LLC), read by the NI−9213 module, were placed at different positions within the bath away from the device to monitor bath temperature. The device power and junction temperature measurements were recorded using the NI−9229 module, with data acquisition and control implemented through a LabVIEW interface with a sampling rate of 2 Hz.

An SJ silicon MOSFET power device (Infineon, IPDQ60T017S7) is used in this study as the heating source for evaluating immersion cooling performance, as well as the electronic component for evaluating electrical insulation performance [77]. This device features a transistor suitable for low frequency switching and high-pulse current applications such as solid relays and line rectification. The package has a top-cooled design with a cooling surface (drain tab) of about 10 mm × 12 mm, ideal for testing immersion cooling of electronics. Most importantly, the device features an embedded temperature sensor for accurate junction temperature measurement, which is critical for thermal performance characterization. Previous immersion cooling studies using electronic devices struggled with accurate junction temperature measurement as they relied on unreliable surface-mounted temperature sensors or time-consuming calibration of device resistance [78–80].

The Infineon device is shown in Fig. 5(a). The device was soldered onto a custom-designed laminate PCB as shown in Fig. 5(b, c). The PCB employs traces for gate control (G and DS), drain (D), and power source (PS) for connecting corresponding pins of the device. Kelvin connections (KPS, KD, and KDS) were also implemented for direct voltage readings to prevent the voltage drops across the traces. The T connection was used for measuring the forward voltage (VF) across the integrated temperature sensor, which exhibits a linear relation to the junction temperature and was calibrated before tests. During the experiment, the transistor gate voltage (Vgs) was set as a constant at 5 V while the drain-source current (Ids) increased step by step, resulting in an increasing power operation of the device. The experiment was halted when the device junction temperature reached 140 °C, maintaining a safe margin below the manufacturer rated limit of 150°C [77].

A total of 10 commercial dielectric fluids were selected for testing in this study. This comprehensive list includes commonly used fluids in immersion cooling, such as silicon oils, fossil derived synthetic hydrocarbons such as polyalphaolefins (PAOs), ester-based oils, and fluorinated fluids. A well-established approach to defining hydrocarbon fluids in this area is by using the API classification system, which classifies base stocks via their production, composition and viscometric performance [81]. Group I to III are refined from crude oil whereas group IV are fully synthetic produced fluids such as PAOs; group V contains anything that does not fall into groups I-IV. We have used this approach to describe where the fluids tested fall into this classification. Generally, hydrocarbons fluids which fall into groups I to IV are complex mixtures of compounds and not a discrete molecular structure. In addition, 5 potential organic fluid candidates were also tested to explore the potential of organic fluids with different distinct molecular structures, including tetradecane, bis(2-ethylhexyl) adipate, diethylene glycol dibutyl ether, 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene, and methyl salicylate. The selected list represents a diverse range of dielectric liquids with different chemistries (hydrocarbons, ester-based, silicone-based, fluorinated fluids), as well as other commercially unavailable organic compounds with thermophysical properties that suggest high performance in immersion cooling. The set of dielectrics tested includes both commercially deployed liquids for benchmarking the current practice, and candidate chemistries with distinct molecular structures for exploring performance limits and formulation opportunities. Table 1 shows a summary of the thermophysical properties of the tested fluids measured at 25°C.

The Infineon device employs an integrated on-chip temperature sensor based on the forward voltage characteristics of a series of three diodes. Located close to the active cells of the semiconductor die, the sensor provides reliable junction temperature readings and faster response during transient tests compared to conventional techniques. The relation between forward voltage (VF) across the temperature sensor and junction temperature (Tj) was calibrated in the temperature-controlled oven. A constant bias current of 200 µA was applied to the sensor using a combination of an Agilent 6623A DC power supply set to 8 V and current source module REF200 (Texas Instruments [82]). A T-type thermocouple placed close to the device was used for reference temperature measurement. Fig. 6 shows the calibration curve of two different devices (1 and 2). For each test, the temperature inside the oven was changed step by step, allowing the voltage reading and reference temperature to stabilize to ensure a thermal equilibrium. The device was also powered off during calibration to avoid self-heating. The combined dataset was used to fit a linear correlation between Tj and VF, expressed by:
(10)

Fig. 6 showed that the data of boards 1 and 2 overlapped, and the fitting correlation showed an R-square value of 0.9999, indicating the excellent stability of the temperature sensing.

During the thermal performance test, the source-drain current
I
ds
was controlled by a variable DC power supply (ATO-JK3060K) to incrementally raise the device temperature in steps of approximately 10°C. After each current step, steady state was determined when the junction temperature stabilized to within a minimum standard deviation (< 0.1° C). After each cycle, the PCB was removed and cleaned using acetone and compressed air to remove any residual fluid. The same cleaning procedure was applied to the stainless-steel coil before switching to the next fluid to avoid contamination. Each dielectric fluid was tested under identical conditions. Uncertainty in measured parameters was accounted for by using standard statistical methods during post-processing. Experimental fluid properties were evaluated with junction temperature (Tj) ranging from 20 to 140 °C and fluid bulk temperature of Tf = 20 °C.

The current leakage procedure measures the leakage current through the Infineon device while immersed in the various dielectric liquids. The test uses the same Infineon device with the transistor in the OFF state. In the OFF-state configuration, the gate and source terminals of the transistor are shorted (Vgs = 0 V). These shorted terminals are connected in series with the digital multimeter (DMM), a power supply, and a current‐limiting resistor (120 kΩ) as shown in Fig. 7. The negative terminal of the power supply is connected to the gate/source, and the positive terminal is connected to the drain. This ensures the transistor is biased in the OFF state, where any significant leakage current indicates failure or poor insulation. In real applications, this OFF configuration describes the leakage current through a transistor switch when it is not supposed to conduct (nanoampere range). If there is ineffective insulation or if the package has defects, higher currents may flow through the dielectric fluids rather than remaining negligible. Additionally, because the voltage is applied across terminals of the same device, the distance the leakage current must travel through the dielectric liquid is relatively small; in this case, 17 mm.

This configuration is derived from standard Surface Insulation Resistance (SIR) tests used in industry to detect short circuits in tightly spaced conductor traces [83–85]. The current leakage test was conducted immediately after thermal testing to assess dielectric insulation and fluid compatibility under electrical stress [66]. Test voltages of 100 V, 300 V, and 550 V were applied sequentially, and current data were recorded at 1 Hz using the LabVIEW interface through Keysight 34465. A digital multimeter transient current was recorded for 3 min after the voltage was applied. The average steady state leakage current was then calculated. Transient data was also plotted to compare the steady state leakage current and stabilization time across fluids.

The instrument specifications to measure the temperature and voltage drop associated with the device, shunt resistance, and the temperature sensor diode were directly adopted as the uncertainty limits for all the input parameters. The individual contributions for each of the parameters were combined through the Root Sum Squared (RSS) method. The adopted limits for each of the measured input parameters are shown in Table 2.

Limit values from Table 2 were used to compute the final average uncertainty associated with all data points for the tested fluids, as summarized in Table 3.

Fig. 8 compares the steady state junction-to-bath temperature rise (ΔT = Tj -Tf) of the Infineon device as a function of electrical power input ( Q ) for all 15 dielectric fluids. The slope of each trace is the effective thermal resistance according to Eq. (15) and provides a direct metric of cooling efficacy which will be discussed later. According to the figure, three performance tiers emerge. Tier 1 includes MS, DGDE, TD, and TCB (see Table 1 for fluids), which exhibit the flattest slopes, holding ΔT below 120°C even as power approaches 25 W, which translates to Rt ≈ 5
K / W
(Eq. 15). These liquids pair low dynamic viscosity with comparatively high thermal conductivity, enhancing the Rayleigh number and convective heat transfer coefficient as predicted by the FOM analysis in Section II. Tier 2, which consists of SO−5, PAO, PO, SPHO, SE, DEHA, and PFPE clusters around Rt ≈ 6
K / W ,
while tier 3 includes SO−20, MO−12, MO−24, and SGF, showing the steepest slopes (Rt > 7
K / W
). The experimental hierarchy, therefore, aligns with the weight-factor rankings, where the best coolants are those that simultaneously maximize liquid thermal conductivity (k) and density (ρ) while minimizing dynamic viscosity (μ).

Physically, the total thermal resistance between the junction and fluid comprises conduction through the package and convection into the dielectric fluid. The thin, highly conductive drain tab offers a shorter conduction path thereby making package resistance negligible compared to fluid-side resistance. The convective heat transfer performance for the dielectric fluids was then quantified through the corresponding natural convection heat transfer coefficient. In practice, estimation of the actual wetted area is nontrivial since the device geometry involves multiple irregular 3D surfaces. Therefore, the vertical drain copper tab as shown in Fig. 5(a) with a well-defined geometry was selected as the reference surface for evaluating heat transfer coefficient. The fraction of total heat dissipated through the drain tab was estimated through steady-state thermal simulation in ANSYS, which were validated against the experimentally measured junction-to-fluid temperature against device power input curves for 3 representative fluids each selected from a different performance tier. The validation ensured heat flow portioning accurately captured physical system behavior. The experimental net heat dissipated through the drain tab, QCu, was estimated to be around 26% of the total heating power, Q, over the temperature difference range from 20 ℃ to 120 ℃ . The detailed simulation setup, mesh independence, and validation are provided in Appendix A.

Fig. 10 shows the variation of the heat transfer coefficient on the copper drain tab with temperature difference for all the tested fluids. The heat transfer coefficient increases consistently with increasing temperature, primarily because reduced viscosity near the heated device surface promotes stronger buoyancy-driven natural convection flows. Since the bulk fluid is maintained nearly around 21°C by the external chiller, this natural convection loop is much stronger at higher junction temperatures since the induced convection circulation draws colder fluids from the bulk tank back to the device effectively increasing overall convective heat transfer. Tier 1 candidate fluids show the highest heat transfer coefficient approximately 15–20% improvement over Tier 2 and 40–50% higher performance relative to Tier 3. Within commercial fluids, Tier 2 outperform tier 3 by 20–30% corresponding to an overall lower thermal resistance range. For reference, the theoretical water natural convection heat transfer coefficient over a vertical plate calculated with the Churchill and Chu [72] correlation is roughly three to five times higher than the best dielectrics fluids tested, highlighting the superior thermal transport capability of water. However, water’s electrical conductivity and risk of corrosion make it unsuitable for direct immersion cooling applications. While conformal insulation coating on electronic devices allows direct water-based cooling, such coatings introduce additional thermal resistance due to the lower thermal conductivity of the coating and affect the potential heat transfer benefits.

Fig. 11(b) compares the resulting

with

, showing strong agreement within ±10% across all 9 fluids. This confirms the validity of using experimentally measured
R
t
to estimate
FOM
t
without requiring full property data with the change of temperature. Such a correlation is particularly valuable because obtaining complete temperature-dependent property data for commercial dielectric fluids and mixtures remains costly and time-consuming. The ability to directly infer
FO
M
exp
from thermal resistance measurements allows rapid and consistent benchmarking of SPIC fluids. Moreover, while theoretical FOMs are intuitive and property-based, the experimental framework captures practical effects such as structural materials and heat-spreading characteristics of real electronic assemblies. Therefore, this correlation provides a robust link between experimental testing and theoretical evaluation offering a convenient pathway to assess, compare, and select dielectric fluids even when detailed material data are unavailable. In addition, the present framework can be extended to pumped SPIC configurations by incorporating controlled flow rates and pressure drop measurements.

Fig. 12 records the transient leakage current response when a voltage bias is first applied to the immersed, off-state Infineon device in SPHO. Three applied voltages of 100, 300, and 550 V were recorded, along with one where no bias was applied. For the non-zero biases,
I
l
shows a rapid initial decay over the first 10–30 s and approaches a quasi-steady plateau. The plateau level increases with applied voltage for about 1.2 nA at 100 V, 2 nA at 300 V, and 5 nA at 550 V. Even at 550 V, the currents remain in the single digit nA range, highlighting the high resistivity of SPHO liquid. The 0 V trace oscillates around zero and represents the measurement noise floor of the current leakage setup (instrumentation and cabling), setting the resolution of current leakage to 50 pA stated in Table 3. The average of the current leakage at each bias voltage was reported in Fig. 13 for every tested fluid.

Fig. 13 plots the closed switch leakage current for each fluid at 100 V, 300 V, and 550 V on a logarithmic scale. For most of the commercial candidates like silicon oils, SPHO, PAO, PO, MO−12, MO−24, SE, SGF, and TD, the bars lie below 10 nA at all different voltages applied. This consistent level of low current response is attributed to the intrinsic high resistivity of pure base oils (silicon, PFPE, white mineral oil) and the stabilizing effect of additive compounds (oxidation inhibitors or metal deactivators) in the formulated fluids. Collectively, these properties ensure robust dielectric behavior and minimal leakage even at the highest bias. In contrast, two fluids display markedly higher conduction. MS has the highest leakage at approximately 0.04 mA when 550 V is applied. The second one, TCB, exhibited approximately 0.1694 μA at 550 V. Overall, most fluids maintain tight leakage control across the operating range, whereas the polar or halogenated liquids (MS, DEHA, TCB) exhibit a steeper voltage sensitivity that could require additional insulation strategies in high voltage applications.

Table 4 summarizes the twin figures of merit for thermal and electrical performance, Rt at ΔT ≈ 120⁰C and
I
l
at 550 V, respectively. Amongst the tested fluids, the thermal resistance value ranges from 4.9 K/W for MS to 7.8 K/W for SGF, which indicates a large variation in heat dissipation capability for SPIC natural convection cooling while maintaining the same fluid bulk liquid temperature. While multiple fluids exhibit leakage current < 5 nA, thermally better performing organic fluids such as MS and TCB show significantly higher leakage current, which suggests limitations with dielectric insulation properties. Amongst other pure fluids, DEHA and DGDE exhibit relatively low thermal resistance with lower leakage current values. TD offers the most favorable combination of low thermal resistance (5.2 K/W) and low leakage current (4.5 nA), making it a well-balanced performer amongst the pure fluids in both the thermal and electrical aspects involved in SPIC.

Furthermore, the volume resistivity of each fluid can be estimated from the measured leakage current values, providing a quantitative metric for comparing dielectric strength across candidates. The detailed methodology and corresponding resistivity calculations are provided in the Appendix B. Notably, according to the Open Compute Project (OCP) base specification for immersion fluids [86], a minimum volume resistivity of 109 Ω·m is recommended for dielectric fluids intended for data center immersion applications.

A systematic comparative analysis of 15 dielectric liquids for single-phase immersion cooling (SPIC) was conducted through combined thermal and electrical performance testing. The property sensitivity and weight-factor framework established here confirmed that maximizing liquid thermal conductivity and liquid density while minimizing liquid viscosity is critical to enhancing natural convection performance in SPIC systems. Experimental results demonstrated that liquids with inherently low dynamic viscosity and moderate to high thermal conductivity, such as methyl salicylate, tetradecane, and diethylene glycol dibutyl ether, achieved superior thermal resistance values under steady state conditions. Tier 1 candidate liquids showed characteristic thermal resistances of 4.9 - 5.4 K/W (2.32 – 2.56 (cm2ˑK)/W), compared with 5.9 - 6.4 K/W (2.82 – 3.08 (cm2ˑK)/W) for the best commercial tier 2 liquids (15–20% lower), and 7.0 - 7.8 K/W (3.27 – 3.75 (cm2ˑK)/W) for tier 3 commercial options (30–36% lower). Electrical insulation measurements showed that the majority of commercial liquids maintained leakage currents of 3.7 −5 nA at 550 V, while certain polar or halogenated compositions exhibited higher electrical conduction. For example, 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene exhibited 169.4 nA of current leakage, and methyl salicylate was at 40,065.3 nA, which were 34-, and 8000-times higher relative to the 5 nA commercial liquid baseline, respectively, which highlights application dependent trade-offs in dielectric selection. Overall, the results emphasize that liquid choice has a significant impact on SPIC system performance and reliability, and the methodology presented here offers a robust pathway for evaluating and optimizing dielectric liquids for advanced electronics cooling applications.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The vertical drain tab surface (
12 mm × 10.3 mm
) was selected as the reference area to estimate the heat transfer coefficient of the dielectric fluids. The coefficient was calculated using
(A.1)

where
Q
Cu
is the net heat dissipated through the drain tab and Atab is the copper tab surface area. However, since there are other exposed surfaces of the device such as epoxy, and copper lead plate, in the total heat dissipation ( Q ) term,
Q
Cu
represents only a fraction of this total power dissipation.

To quantify this fraction, 3D steady state CFD simulations were conducted in ANSYS Fluent. Detailed structural and geometry information, including drain tab, epoxy package, solder, die, and PCB laminate based on manufacturer datasheet were incorporated to simulate a well-defined domain. The internal structure of the Infineon PG-HDSOP package used in this study is illustrated in Figure A1(a). The device consists of silicon die (5 mm × 8 mm × 0.08 mm), where volumetric heat generation is applied. The die is metallized with a top-side copper layer and mounted onto a copper lead frame (drain tab) both of which serve as the primary heat- spreading path. The assembly is encapsulated in an epoxy molding compound. The drain tab and source pins are soldered directly onto the PCB copper pads using standard reflow soldering. No additional thermal interface material was used between the device and PCB. Most of the heat transfer from the device is through top side copper, drain tab, epoxy, and copper solder. Two fluids, MS and DEHA with quite different thermal properties were chosen for this simulation. The dielectric fluid properties- density, viscosity, specific heat capacity, and thermal conductivity were defined as temperature dependent functions using the user defined.scm file to accurately capture property variation with temperature in the domain during simulation. The fluid-contacting walls were assigned a constant temperature boundary condition of 294.15 K to represent the externally chilled bulk fluid in the experiment. The die was defined as a volumetric heat source, with heat generation calculated from the electrical power input and die dimensions of
8 mm × 5 mm × 0.08 mm
. Table A1 shows the summary of the simulation model.

The boundary conditions used in the natural convection thermal simulation as shown in Table A1 include the initial ambient/ bulk temperature set to 294.15 K (21⁰C). External exposed surfaces were subjected to a convective heat transfer with uniform heat transfer coefficient of h = 10 W/(m2 .K). The bottom tank wall was modelled as an adiabatic wall. A constant temperature boundary condition of 294.14 K (21⁰C) was imposed on the interior walls of the tank to represent the ideal thermal sink condition maintained by the stainless-steel coiled heat exchanger surrounding the PCB in the experimental system. Volumetric heat generation as expressed by eq. (A.2), was applied with the silicon die (Vol = 5 mm ×8 mm ×0.08 mm).
(A.2)
q
′ ′ ′
=
Q
Vol
Table A1
Summary of the simulation parameters used in the ANSYS Fluent model.
Model setting Description Notes
Mesh 3427,365 poly- polyhedral elements, refined near the solid-fluid interface. Validated by mesh independence.
Heat source Volumetric heat source from the die inside the device. Die size of 8 mm× 5 mm× 0.08 mm, given by the product info
Boundary conditions 1. Operating pressure of 1 atm
2. Constant fluid-contacting walls
3. Temperature boundary condition of 294.15 K
4. h = 10 W/(m2ˑK) for top and PCB outer walls
5. Adiabatic bottom wall The constant temperature wall boundary approximates well-regulated thermal reservoir

Figure A1(a) shows the 3-D computational domain that consists of the Infineon power device with detailed internal layers including copper drain tab, solder, die, epoxy package frame, and PCB laminate immersed in the dielectric test fluid. Figure A1(b) shows the mesh and mesh refinement near the copper tab of interest. A polyhedral mesh was selected for this slightly complicated geometry to reduce solving time and improve accuracy. Local mesh sizing was applied individually to critical components of the package and the surrounding fluid domain, ensuring targeted refinement in regions where large gradients in temperature and velocity are possible. The simulations were conducted using a steady state, pressure-based solver in ANSYS Fluent under laminar flow conditions considering fluid as Newtonian and incompressible. Gravity was included to model buoyancy driven natural convection with thermophysical properties defined as fully temperature dependent functions to accurately capture temperature gradient effects in the system domain. The coupled pressure-velocity scheme was used with PRESTO! discretization for pressure and second order upwind schemes for momentum and energy equations. Convergence was verified through residual reduction below 10−3 and stabilization of maximum temperature of the silicon die and heat flux values.

The simulations were performed by solving the steady-state three-dimensional conservation equations for mass, momentum, and energy. The governing equations are solved in the domain are stated, along with the assumptions of steady-state, laminar, incompressible flow with temperature dependent thermophysical properties. Buoyancy effects were modeled by including gravity and fully temperature dependent density. These clarify mathematical framework of the methods used for modelling.

The temperature-dependent property correlations for density, thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, viscosity, and thermal expansion coefficient are summarized in Table A2, Table A3, Table A4, Table A5, and Table A6 respectively. These properties were used for the predictive analysis of the Churchill and Chu [72] shown in Figure A4. Only nine out of the 15 fluids experimentally evaluated fluids were chosen since complete temperature-dependent property correlations over the entire operating temperature range were available only for these fluids. This ensures a consistent and physically meaningful comparison between the model predictions and experimental data, particularly since these properties vary significantly with temperature, especially viscosity which strongly influences natural convection behavior.

The simulated results for DEHA at 18 W power input illustrate a clear buoyancy-driven convection pattern above the drain tab as shown in Figure A1(c) with which clearly illustrates the strong thermal gradients formed near the drain tab surface relative to the bulk fluid temperature based on input boundary conditions. Three vertical plumes forming from the device center and the solder patch on either side of the package. Figure A1(d) represents the velocity field in the direction perpendicular to the device-PCB assembly, which shows a defined upward jet along the front of the device. The rear side of the PCB has a slightly slower jet due to lower thermal conductivity of the PCB, which creates a temperature gradient from the device-mounted side to the rear surface.
Fig. A1
(a) Three-dimensional simulation domain showing the immersed power device and surrounding fluid region with detailed cross-sectional view of the device stack, and (b) corresponding mesh with mesh refinement in the solid–fluid interface region. (c) Simulated steady-state temperature distribution across the device and surrounding fluid-DEHA at 18 W. (d) Corresponding velocity field showing natural convection around the heated device.’.
.

Figure A2 presents the mesh independence study for the fraction of total heat dissipated through the drain tab
( Q
Cu
/ Q )
as a function of the number of mesh elements.
Q
Cu
is obtained by calculating the total heat from the drain tab surface. This is computed by using the local heat flux value over the tab area estimated from the simulation. The solution converges beyond approximately 1.7 million elements with maximum variation in fraction less than 0.93% satisfying 1% mesh convergence criteria for the chosen mesh size of approximately 3.4 million elements as highlighted in Figure A2 and detailed in Table A1.

Figure A3 shows the validation of the CFD model against experimental data for the two fluids DEHA and MS. The simulated junction-to-fluid temperature rise matches closely with experimental data, with an average deviation of less than 7% across the testes power range. The minor deviations between numerical and experimental results for DEHA may arise from experimental uncertainties, sensitivity of natural convection to temperature dependent properties such as viscosity, and the idealized boundary condition assumptions in the CFD model. Since DEHA exhibits stronger viscosity variation with temperature than MS, small property deviations can lead to amplified differences in predicted buoyancy driven heat transfer. This agreement confirms that the model effectively captures both the conduction through different layers of the electronic device package and natural convection driven by temperature-dependent fluid properties for the selected fluids.
Fig. A2
Mesh independence study showing the fraction of total heat dissipated through the drain tab
( Q
Cu
/ Q )
as a function of the number of mesh elements. The dashed circle shows the mesh used for analysis.
Fig. A3
Comparison between experimental and simulated junction to ambient temperature rise as a function of heating power for 2 dielectric fluids DEHA and MS.
.

Results from the fluids indicate that
Q
Cu
accounts for approximately 25–27% of the total power input, with minor variation at higher junction temperature. As seen in Figure A4, the simulated heat dissipation fraction nearly remains constant with increasing temperature difference, which confirms the relative contribution by the copper drain tab to heat removal is largely independent of the operating power. Accordingly, a general correlation for estimating the heat dissipation through the copper tab is given in eq. (A.4), with an accuracy of ± 3% for both MS and DEHA across the tested temperature range.

Based on this modification of the
Q

= 0.26 Q
, we validate this estimation method by comparing the experimental HTC (
h
Exp .
) with the predicted HTC (
h
Pred .
) from the Churchill and Chu [72] correlation for natural convection of vertical surfaces using Eq. (4) for a constant heat flux boundary condition. Evaluating the thermophysical properties of the dielectric liquids at liquid film temperature, the predictive capability of the Churchill and Chu [72] correlation is shown in Figure A5 for nine liquids spanning all three tiers, viz., Tier 1 (TCB, MS, TD, and DGDE), Tier 2 (PO, PAO, and DEHA), and Tier 3 (SGF and MO−24). For this analysis, only the 9 fluids used in Fig. 11 with accurately known temperature-dependent thermophysical properties obtained from the manufacturer or literature sources were considered to ensure a reliable comparison. For the combined liquid dataset with 108 data points, with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 10.2%, this correlation captures 88% and 97% of the data within ±20% error and ±30% error, respectively.

.

Given the device geometries, it is difficult to determine the exact cross-sectional area and length of the current leakage path. Instead, an effective value of l/A is estimated by assuming:
1)
The transistor resistance, Rtransistor, is only dependent on the device characteristics and applied voltage and device and does not change when immersed in different fluids.
2)
The effective current leakage pathway remains the same for different fluids.
